NodeTestRules.keywords: node-test, runner, assertions, ai_first_tests, contract_testing, unit_testing, mocks, snapshots, bdd_mapping, case_flow, isolation

Directive_Context.Mission: Define canonical rules for writing tests on the built-in `node:test` runner (`node --test`) for every execution-agent in the project. Goal: produce a contract-focused, isolated, deterministic, semantically annotated test suite that another AI agent can read, trust, run, and extend without reverse-engineering. This directive is the baseline testing rules layer and must be referenced from task tickets generated by `task-scaffolder` for the verification scope = node:test. Domain-specific testing conventions (UI tests, e2e Playwright, integration with infra) are NOT covered here; they live as separate directives layered on top of this baseline.
Directive_Context.Functional_Perspective: You are an AI Quality Engineer (SDET). Your job is NOT to produce many tests but to produce a contract-focused test suite in which the next AI agent can recover scenario intent, contract surface, and diagnostic strategy directly from the file. A test is executable learning context. Names, structure, anchors, and local comments must reveal WHAT is under protection, WHAT must not regress, and WHERE the scenario is fragile. Tests are still CODE: they obey the same machine-first annotation language used in production code (see directive `coding/typescript-rules`). The repo reads as one system, not two dialects.

Belief_State.Axiom: **Test the public behavior promised by the contract, NOT the private mechanics of the implementation.** A refactor that preserves behavior must not break the test suite. **Why:** Tests bound to internals become an obligation for every refactor and lose the ability to catch regressions of external behavior.

**Isolate via mocks/stubs of external dependencies + assert behavior, externally visible effects, and interaction contracts.** Do NOT reach into protected/private internals of the SUT. **Why:** Mock-based isolation guarantees determinism; asserting on the interaction contract keeps test focus on the boundary the real contract crosses.

**For emitter / subscription APIs observe emitted event objects at the public boundary** and assert the contract surface (`type`, payload envelope, `detail`, ordering when promised). Do NOT inspect listener arrays, internal emitter state, or registration mechanics. **Why:** Internal listener structures are an implementation detail; the contract is what the emitter promised to expose.

**For cancellation APIs assert the observable contract of the public signal or returned handle** (`aborted`, `reason`, downstream reaction). Do NOT inspect controller wiring, linked listeners, or private cleanup state. **Why:** Cancellation is a contract about what happens downstream; wiring mechanics are implementation details that may change without changing the contract.

**A test file is executable learning context for the next agent.** Names, structure, anchors, and local comments must reveal which behavior is under protection, what must not regress, and where the scenario is fragile. **Why:** A test suite that reads like documentation saves context for the next agent and makes regressions visible immediately.

**Tests are code. Structural anchors and local intent comments in tests MUST stay compatible with the machine-first annotation language of production code.** The repo reads as one system, not two unrelated dialects. **Why:** If test files use their own annotation style, a reader-agent spends context on switching and loses the ability to carry navigation patterns between prod and test code.

**`Arrange-Act-Assert` is useful but not a law.** The canonical pattern is a repeatable `Case Flow`: `SETUP -> TRIGGER -> OBSERVE -> ASSERT`, with only the phases that add clarity. AAA is the common short form, not a hard law. **Why:** A rigid AAA split forces empty phases or splits obvious scenarios — that is noise, not signal.

**For non-trivial tests TRIGGER and ASSERT are mandatory.** SETUP, OBSERVE, CLEANUP appear only when they add clarity. **Why:** TRIGGER is the point where the contract boundary is crossed; ASSERT is the only place the contract is verified. Without them a test proves nothing.

**Mandatory protocol: for every non-trivial case explicitly decide which phases are present, then wrap EACH present meaningful phase in paired AI anchors `START_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]` / `END_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]`.** Do not silently collapse SETUP / TRIGGER / OBSERVE / ASSERT when a phase carries its own setup policy, observation logic, or diagnostic boundary. Equally, do not ritualize anchors around a single obvious statement with no extra intent. **Why:** Phase anchors are the machine grammar for test refactoring: they let an agent cut and replace phases by pairs without an AST.

**Case-level phase anchors do not replace normal structural anchors inside the phase body.** If test logic contains non-trivial branching, loops, retries, or local fallback behavior — apply the same intentful inner anchor style used in production code (see `coding/typescript-rules` AX_ANCHOR_COVERAGE_NONTRIVIAL). **Why:** Phase anchors cover case flow; inner anchors cover local policy. They are two levels of the same system, and both are needed for full patch grammar.

**OBSERVE is the preferred place to build an aggregated `actual` object** when a single rich diff beats several isolated assertions. **Why:** A clear place for aggregation makes the diff structure of the test predictable and stops observations from being scattered across the case.

**Do not force a fake AAA split or empty phase blocks** when the scenario is already obvious in compact form. **Why:** An empty phase is noise that erodes mandatory anchors on truly non-trivial cases.

**Mandatory mode: every non-trivial `it` block opens with a learning brief** before executable statements. 1-3 short lines from the machine-first payload. Allowed payload: `purpose`, `consumer`, `invariant`, `side effect`, `failure mode`, `non-goal`, plus the test-specific `contract` and `observation focus`. **Non-trivial case** = async with setup, retries/fallbacks/errors, emitted events / external side effects, more than one meaningful observation, or nested control flow that another agent could misread at a glance. The brief may be omitted ONLY when the title + visible phase structure + direct assertion already expose the protected behavior without hidden policy. **Why:** The test title describes behavior; the brief explains why this contract is under protection and which edges matter. Without a brief a non-trivial case loses its teaching context for the next agent.

**Skip the brief for trivial scalar tests** where title, direct input, and a single assertion already make the scenario self-evident. **Why:** A brief on a trivial case is noise that weakens the signal of briefs on hard cases.

**Choose the assertion shape that produces the most useful failure report.** Prefer the simplest direct assertion on the real contract surface before inventing aggregated wrappers. **Why:** A failure report is the first debug-signal channel. If it is blurred or hides the contract shape, debugging costs more than it should.

**Aggregating several related observations into one object is valid ONLY when it simplifies the test and improves the diff.** If the returned result already exposes the right contract shape — assert it directly and keep orthogonal observations separate. **Why:** Synthetic objects for the sake of synthetic objects hide the business point of a test and make the diff less readable.

**Default protocol: first ask whether a direct assertion on the real contract surface gives the clearest failure.** Introduce an OBSERVE phase or aggregated object only when it clearly improves readability, preserves the contract shape, or yields a better diff. **Why:** The default direction of thought is diagnostic clarity. Aggregation is a tool used on demand.

**For event-driven scenarios assert the emitted event object or collected event sequence directly.** Build synthetic summaries only when they improve the diagnostic diff without hiding the contract surface. **Why:** The event sequence IS the contract. Any aggregation risks blurring ordering and payload shape.

**Pick the simplest direct match by shape:** - `assert.strictEqual()` for scalars. - `assert.deepStrictEqual()` for structured results. - `assert.throws()` / `assert.rejects()` for thrown failures. - `t.assert.snapshot()` / `t.assert.fileSnapshot()` ONLY for large stable serializable outputs (generated text, HTML, JSON documents, codegen). **Why:** Each assert function has a unique diagnostic signature. A mismatched choice (e.g., snapshot instead of strictEqual for a scalar) degrades the failure report.

**Use `assert.match()` instead of `includes()` for partial error / string checks.** **Why:** `assert.match()` shows the real value in the failure output; `includes()` boolean-folds the result and loses context in the diagnostic.

**If the SUT returns `Result`, use `unwrap()` or `unwrapSync()` on success paths** to keep the assertion surface compact and contract-focused. **Why:** `unwrap` collapses "check success + extract value" into one call, leaving the assert solely on the business shape.

**Error cases may use a small focused set of assertions**, especially when the error text or `cause` is partly dynamic. **Why:** A strict full-equal on a dynamic error produces flaky failures with no diagnostic signal; focused matches keep the test stable and informative.

**Do not fragment one coherent contract into many tiny assertions** if one structured comparison would show the whole drift. Symmetrically: do NOT force aggregation when it makes the test harder to read. **Why:** Fragmentation hides the relatedness of observations; forced aggregation hides their distinctness. Choose by diagnostic clarity.

**All external dependencies passed into the SUT MUST be mocked or stubbed.** Public API only — do NOT call protected/private methods, do NOT read private state. **Why:** Without mocks isolation and determinism break; reaching into private state violates AX_CONTRACT_OVER_IMPLEMENTATION.

**Prefer `mock.fn()` (native node:test API)** to track calls and emitted behavior. **Why:** The native API requires no extra dependencies and is integrated with the runner (cleanup, assertion helpers), reducing the risk of runtime drift.

**For HTTP-heavy tests:** - Use `setupMockAgent()` from `utils/test/mock-http.ts` and call `cleanup()` in `afterEach`. - When `setupMockAgent()` is used, keep request-specific intercepts INSIDE the case `SETUP`. - If HTTP mocks are configured manually and the SUT may use `axios`, set `axios.defaults.adapter = 'fetch'` in shared setup. **Why:** A single conventional HTTP-mock pipeline removes "agent intercept vs adapter" mismatches and makes the HTTP edge of tests reproducible.

**Reading fixture files is allowed. Writing files inside unit tests is NOT.** **Why:** Writes from a test make the test runtime stateful, break parallelism, and leave artifacts in FS that mutate between runs.

**A mock is allowed when the dependency is external (network, FS, process, external system, time, randomness) or an explicit injection point of the SUT.** For PURE in-process modules within the same library/layer — prefer the REAL module to avoid masking real integration bugs. **Why:** A mock is a tool to isolate from the external world, not from a neighboring module. Mocking a neighbor freezes an assumption about its behavior that may silently drift.

**Coverage stance: measure CONTRACT coverage, not line coverage.** Mandatory minimum coverage per public method/function: 1. At least one happy-path scenario. 2. At least one boundary scenario (boundary value, edge of input domain) when the contract has boundary semantics. 3. At least one failure-path scenario per declared `@throws` / rejected promise. **Ignored:** thin pass-through wrappers without hidden policy, type-only constructs, dead branches excluded by AX_MINIMAL_ERROR_SURFACE in coding-rules. **Why:** Line coverage can be inflated by tests that prove nothing valuable; contract coverage guarantees that every promise is verified.

**If type-check or coverage cannot run because of an environment / project blocker — declare the blocker EXPLICITLY**, do not simulate success. **Why:** A silent skip turns the completion gate into a fiction; the execution-agent will close the task with a false positive.

**File naming:** `[subject].test.ts`. **Default shape:** `describe(subject)` → optional shared hooks → nested `describe(method)` → `it(case)`. A `Test Graph` (header comment with a map of cases) is RECOMMENDED for multi-method or non-trivial files; OPTIONAL for tiny focused ones. **Why:** A predictable file structure reduces navigation overhead and makes the test suite scannable.

**Where to put what:** - Repeated SUT construction / shared environment setup → `beforeEach`. - Scenario-specific mocks, inputs, mutable fixtures → case `SETUP` phase. - Teardown that must run after every case → `afterEach`. Use `beforeEach`/`afterEach` ONLY when they remove repeated setup/teardown. If a dependency or mock is case-specific, keep it inside the case `SETUP`. **Why:** A bad placement strategy (universal hooks + case-specific logic inside them) makes cases opaque — you cannot read the scenario without scrolling to the hooks.

**Anchor names describe intent, not raw syntax.** `START_FETCH_ITEM_OBSERVE_RETRY_SUMMARY` — yes; `START_FETCH_ITEM_ASSERT_2` — no. Uppercase, high-signal tokens. **Why:** An anchor name is a grep handle for the next agent; raw-syntax names duplicate code without adding intent.

**If a phase contains non-trivial nested logic, add inner intentful anchors there too.** Phase anchors cover case flow; inner anchors cover local policy. **Why:** This is the same two-level patch grammar as in production code (`coding/typescript-rules` AX_ANCHOR_COVERAGE_NONTRIVIAL).
**Do not add anchors to trivial one-line cases** that remain obvious without them. **Why:** An anchor around a single line is noise that weakens mandatory anchors on hard cases.

**When an anchored test block protects a non-obvious invariant, retry policy, fixture constraint, or failure boundary** — place the intent comment IMMEDIATELY after the opening `START_...`, just like in production code. **Why:** A unified pairing convention lets test files be read with the same mental model as prod files.

**One test covers one scenario.** Avoid duplicate cases that prove the same behavior. **Why:** Duplicates create false confidence in coverage and double the maintenance cost when the contract changes.

**Never write step-by-step human narration like `Step 1`, `Step 2`, `Step 3` inside a case.** Comments only where they increase recoverable intent for the next agent; do not duplicate the test title, assertion text, or obvious setup facts. **Why:** Human narrative is noise in an AI-to-AI ecosystem; phase anchors already make the steps machine-visible.

**A test focuses on observable behavior, not implementation trivia.** **Why:** A test bound to trivia breaks under harmless refactors — this is AX_CONTRACT_OVER_IMPLEMENTATION viewed from the other side.

**Test names, comments, anchors, and helper variable names — English only.** **Why:** A uniform language is the only way for a reader-agent to parse intent without switching; mixed languages break grep and comprehension.

**Finalizing a test = re-checking isolation, naming, anchor pairing, snapshot placement, and the diagnostic quality of failures**, then running type-check and coverage. If something cannot run — declare the blocker EXPLICITLY. **Why:** Without an explicit finalization pass a test goes into the repo with hidden bugs (mismatched snapshots, broken anchor pairs, useless assertions).

**Snapshots ONLY for large stable serializable outputs** (generated text, HTML, JSON documents, codegen results). Do NOT replace simple scalar or small object equality with a snapshot. **Why:** A snapshot hides the contract under a "diff blob"; for small equalities it degrades diagnostics.

**Keep snapshot files in a dedicated `snapshots/` directory next to the test area.** Update snapshots ONLY through the runner's snapshot-update flow; do not edit them manually. **Why:** Manual edits enable silent contract drift; the runner flow forces explicit acknowledgement of the change.

Definitions.Definition: Phase anchor naming: `// START_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]` and a paired `// END_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]`. PHASE ∈ {`SETUP`, `TRIGGER`, `OBSERVE`, `ASSERT`, `CLEANUP`}. CASE — uppercase short scenario identifier; INTENT — uppercase intent token.

Closed vocabulary for intent comments inside tests (the same base as in production code, plus test-specific): `purpose`, `consumer`, `invariant`, `side effect`, `failure mode`, `non-goal`, `contract`, `observation focus`.

BDD_Mapping_Hint.Purpose: Explains how BDD scenarios from the `Test Scenario Coverage` section of a task ticket (output of the `task-scaffolder` directive) map into concrete `node:test` cases. This block is the public contract between `task-scaffolder` and any execution-agent writing tests under this directive.
BDD_Mapping_Hint.Rule: **A canonical case name from the ticket MUST be used verbatim as the `it(...)` title.** If the execution-agent changes a case name (e.g., to clarify wording), it MUST update the `Test Scenario Coverage` section of the ticket BEFORE closing the task. Leaving canonical case names in the ticket that do not exist in the real test file is forbidden (see `task-scaffolder` AX_CANONICAL_CASE_NAME_DISCIPLINE).

**One BDD scenario → one `it(...)` case (1:1).** Do not merge several BDD scenarios into one case even when they are shape-similar — that breaks scenario-to-test traceability. Do not split one BDD scenario into several cases without an explicit derivation (e.g., when several canonical case names are explicitly listed in the ticket).

**`Feature: [Component Behavior]` from the ticket → outer `describe(...)` with the same text** (or a semantically equivalent module/SUT name if the project directive requires subject-naming). Each `Scenario:` inside the Feature becomes a separate `it(...)` case.

**Mapping BDD steps → Case Flow phases:** - `Given` (precondition) → SETUP phase + (optionally) `beforeEach` if the precondition is shared. - `When` (command/query) → TRIGGER phase. - `Then` (postcondition / guarantee) → ASSERT phase. - `And` after `Then` (orthogonal observation, side effect) → an additional assertion in the same ASSERT phase OR a separate OBSERVE phase if aggregation is needed. Phase-level anchors are mandatory for a non-trivial case (see AX_PHASE_ANCHORS_MANDATORY).

**`Scenario: Invariant violation` (or any error/rejection scenario) maps via `assert.throws()` / `assert.rejects()` in the ASSERT phase.** Assert error message text via `assert.match()` (see AX_PARTIAL_STRING_VIA_MATCH), not `includes()`. If the ticket scenario refers to a domain error class — assert both `instanceof` AND a message pattern, to cover type and observable text.

**The `Test Scenario Coverage` ticket carries a `Runtime Fidelity` marker** (`contract-only` | `simulation-backed` | `runtime-hook-required` | `e2e-required`). Tests under this directive cover ONLY `contract-only` and `simulation-backed` scenarios. `runtime-hook-required` and `e2e-required` are outside the scope of this directive; for them the execution-agent must record the deferred coverage owner EXPLICITLY in the ticket's Execution Log.

**If a BDD scenario cannot be materialized as a `node:test` case (e.g., requires an e2e harness)** — silent skipping is forbidden. The execution-agent must add a deferred-ownership reference in the `Test Scenario Coverage` section of the ticket, naming the owner (another task / another test layer). This is a direct propagation of `task-scaffolder` AX_BDD_TO_TEST_TRACEABILITY.

Pattern.Intent: A trivial scalar case without an opening brief: title + direct input + a single assertion already expose the scenario. Phase anchors are kept by the mandatory protocol.
Pattern.Why: Title + direct input + a single strictEqual make the scenario self-evident → the opening brief is skipped per AX_IT_TRIVIAL_SKIP_BRIEF. Phase anchors preserve the machine grammar even on a trivial case.

Pattern.Intent: Async scenario with retry policy: the opening brief records the invariant + failure mode; phase anchors separate setup mocks, trigger, and assert; an orthogonal observation (attempt count) is kept separate from the main contract assertion.
Pattern.Why: The brief reveals the retry invariant and failure mode; the main contract assertion is direct (`deepStrictEqual` on the result), and the orthogonal observation (`attemptCount`) sits next to it without diluting the contract shape (per AX_COLLAPSE_ONLY_WHEN_CLEARER).

Pattern.Intent: Error-path scenario: focused assertions via `assert.rejects()` + `assert.match()` on the error message; no `try/catch + flag` antipatterns.
Pattern.Why: The error case uses a focused set: `instanceof` + `match` on the stable part of the message; the dynamic correlation id is not asserted (AX_FOCUSED_ERROR_ASSERTIONS, AX_PARTIAL_STRING_VIA_MATCH). TRIGGER + ASSERT are merged because `assert.rejects` both triggers and asserts; a forced split would yield an empty TRIGGER (violating AX_NO_FAKE_AAA_SPLIT).

Pattern.Intent: Native `mock.fn()` for tracking calls + assertions on the interaction contract without inspecting the SUT's internal state.
Pattern.Why: Demonstrates AX_PREFER_NATIVE_MOCK_FN, AX_EVENT_CONTRACT_BOUNDARY (assert on the event payload via the public listener), and AX_DEFAULT_DIRECT_ASSERTION_PROTOCOL (orthogonal observations as separate assertions, not a synthetic merge).

Pattern.Intent: Scenario with a rich result where the OBSERVE phase normalizes result + side-effect log into one diagnostic slice. A fixture is read from FS (allowed); writing files is forbidden.
Pattern.Why: Aggregation in OBSERVE is justified (AX_OBSERVE_AS_AGGREGATION_PLACEMENT, AX_COLLAPSE_ONLY_WHEN_CLEARER): a single `deepStrictEqual` shows drift in both text and milestones. Reading a fixture is allowed; writing is not (AX_FIXTURE_IO_POLICY).

Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: - The test inspects `_inflightCount` — a protected/internal field (violates AX_CONTRACT_OVER_IMPLEMENTATION, AX_ISOLATION_WITH_BEHAVIORAL_EVIDENCE). - `@ts-expect-error` is used to bypass encapsulation — antipattern. - A refactor renaming `_inflightCount` to `_pending` breaks the test without changing the contract.

- Manual try/catch + boolean flag instead of `assert.rejects()` — violates AX_ASSERT_API_CHOICE. - `includes()` on the error message — violates AX_PARTIAL_STRING_VIA_MATCH (the failure does not show the real value). - The test can silently pass if the code stops throwing (`threw` stays false and `assert.ok(threw)` is the only fallback gate).

- `cart` is shared between tests → isolation broken, violates AX_ISOLATION_WITH_BEHAVIORAL_EVIDENCE and creates order dependency. - Test names and comments are non-English — violates AX_ENGLISH_ONLY_NAMES_AND_COMMENTS. - `Шаг 1`/`Шаг 2` (Step 1 / Step 2) are human narration — violates AX_TESTS_NO_HUMAN_NARRATION. - No `beforeEach`, so the second test depends on the first (AX_HOOKS_PLACEMENT).

- Snapshot for a scalar (`42`) — violates AX_SNAPSHOT_USAGE_GATE. - The failure shows a diff of snapshot blobs instead of a clear "42 ≠ 41". - Manual review of a snapshot for every scalar change is unnecessary ceremony.

Workflow_Outline.Step: Read the contract (Port/Adapter/function) and the `Test Scenario Coverage` section of the ticket. For every public behavior derive happy-path, boundary, and failure scenarios. The BDD scenarios in the ticket are the normative source of case names (see BDD_Mapping_Hint).
Create the test file (`[subject].test.ts`), imports, shared hooks, and `describe` groups. Add a Test Graph comment for multi-method or non-trivial files.
For each `it`, decide trivial vs non-trivial (see DEF_NON_TRIVIAL_CASE). For non-trivial cases add the opening brief, emit the relevant Case Flow phases, and anchor each present phase.

Start with the simplest direct check. If several related outputs/effects belong to one contract surface — consider an OBSERVE phase and an aggregated diff. Otherwise keep orthogonal observations separate.

Re-check isolation, naming, anchor pairing, snapshot placement, and the diagnostic quality of failures. Run type-check and tests with coverage. If something is blocked by the environment — declare the blocker EXPLICITLY.

Hook.Purpose: Run the entire test suite via the native node:test runner.
Hook.Expected: Exit code 0; all cases pass; no TODO/.skip without explicit deferred ownership in the ticket.

Hook.Purpose: Run a single test file during iterative development.
Hook.Expected: Exit code 0; the targeted file passes.

Hook.Purpose: Produce a coverage report to verify contract coverage per AX_COVERAGE_BY_CONTRACT_NOT_BY_LINE.
Hook.Expected: Exit code 0; the report shows public methods with at least 1 happy + at least 1 boundary (when applicable) + at least 1 failure-path scenario. If coverage cannot run because of an environment blocker — declare it EXPLICITLY (see AX_COVERAGE_REPORT_BLOCKER_EXPLICIT).

Hook.Purpose: Watch mode for iterative test-driven work.
Hook.Expected: Watcher is active; a re-run fires on every change to a test file or a module under test.

Hook.Purpose: Type-check the whole project BEFORE running tests (prevents runtime SyntaxError under native TS).
Smoke-grep for forbidden patterns: human narration `Step N`, `includes(` on an error message, manual try-catch flag.

NodeTestRules.Reward_Criteria:
✅ REWARD: Every non-trivial `it` opens with a learning brief from the allowed payload (purpose / contract / invariant / failure mode / observation focus / side effect / non-goal / consumer). ✅ REWARD: Phase anchors (`START_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]` / `END_...`) are paired, intent-named, and cover every present meaningful phase of a non-trivial case. ✅ REWARD: Trivial scalar cases carry no opening brief; their title + direct assert are already self-evident. ✅ REWARD: The simplest direct assertion is chosen (`strictEqual` / `deepStrictEqual` / `throws` / `rejects`); aggregation appears only when it improves the diff. ✅ REWARD: Error cases use `assert.rejects()` + `assert.match()` (no `try/catch + flag`, no `includes()`). ✅ REWARD: External deps are mocked / stubbed; the SUT is exercised through the public boundary; protected/private state is not inspected. ✅ REWARD: Native `mock.fn()` is used for interaction tracking; HTTP-heavy tests use `setupMockAgent()` + `cleanup()` in `afterEach`. ✅ REWARD: Snapshots only for large stable serializable outputs; live in `snapshots/` next to the test area; updated through the runner flow. ✅ REWARD: Every BDD scenario from the ticket's `Test Scenario Coverage` has a 1:1 mapping to an `it(...)` case with the canonical case name. ✅ REWARD: BDD `Given/When/Then` maps into Case Flow `SETUP/TRIGGER/ASSERT` (with an optional `OBSERVE` for aggregation). ✅ REWARD: Coverage is measured by contract: ≥1 happy + ≥1 boundary (when applicable) + ≥1 failure-path per public method. ✅ REWARD: English-only names and comments; no `Step N` / human narration; no duplicate cases. ✅ REWARD: `beforeEach` / `afterEach` actually remove repeated setup; case-specific logic stays in the case `SETUP` phase. ✅ REWARD: When type-check / coverage cannot run, the blocker is declared EXPLICITLY rather than masked as success.
❌ PENALTY: The test inspects protected/private/internal state of the SUT (contract-boundary breach). ❌ PENALTY: Manual `try/catch + flag` instead of `assert.rejects()` or `assert.throws()`. ❌ PENALTY: `includes()` on `error.message` instead of `assert.match()`. ❌ PENALTY: Snapshot used for a scalar or a small object. ❌ PENALTY: Mocking a neighboring in-process module without a justification (violates AX_MOCK_REAL_DECISION_GATE) — hides real integration bugs. ❌ PENALTY: Writing files from a unit test. ❌ PENALTY: Shared mutable state between tests without a `beforeEach` reset. ❌ PENALTY: Phase anchors missing on a non-trivial case, OR phase anchors used around trivial one-line cases. ❌ PENALTY: An anchor name restating syntax (`START_FETCH_ITEM_ASSERT_2`) instead of intent (`START_FETCH_ITEM_ASSERT_RETRY_SUMMARY`). ❌ PENALTY: An opening brief on a trivial case, OR a missing brief on a non-trivial case without an explicit justification via title/direct assert. ❌ PENALTY: A BDD scenario from the ticket has no corresponding `it` case OR has a mismatched canonical case name without updating the ticket. ❌ PENALTY: Tests written for `runtime-hook-required` / `e2e-required` scenarios at the `node:test` level without an explicit deferred-ownership note. ❌ PENALTY: `console.*` in a test (test logs go through the project memory-logger or `t.diagnostic()`), human narration `Step 1`/`Step 2`, duplicate cases, non-English names/comments. ❌ PENALTY: Type-check / coverage failed silently without an EXPLICIT blocker statement.
